save_roast_quote.name: save_roast_quote
save_roast_quote.description: Save Donald’s best roast line for the report card (from the roast segment only — not from the post-roast AI survival tips). Call this after Phase 4. After this returns, ask the user a follow-up question — don’t leave the conversation hanging.

parameters.description: Extract Donald’s single best roast one-liner from the monologue he just delivered—the line most likely to be screenshotted. Verbatim from his last roast, as a short string. No session_id.

research_degree.name: research_degree
research_degree.description: Triggers research on the USER'S BACKEND (browser → API → Firecrawl + AI). You do not browse the web yourself. When this tool returns JSON with research_complete true, research is ALREADY DONE — start the roast in your next speech; never ask the user to say research is ready or to confirm the data loaded. The JSON includes safeguard_tips (ordered list of strings = top moves). After the roast, Phase 4: explicitly walk through those moves — explain what to do and briefly why each matters for this user (use ai_risk_reasoning / ai_replacement_risk for context); end with “if you only do one thing…” using the top tip. Then save_roast_quote (roast one-liner only), then follow-ups.

parameters.description: From the live transcript: the user’s major, field of study, or degree name exactly as they stated it (e.g. Communications, Computer Science, MBA). If unclear, infer the closest standard label they implied. Never invent a degree they did not describe.
From the transcript: the college or university name they said they attended. Use their wording. If they only said a state school, put that phrase—do not make up a specific school name.

Four-digit year: use the year they FINISHED if already graduated; if still in school, use EXPECTED graduation year (future year is OK). If unknown, use 0. Digits only when possible so the client can coerce to a number.

Their situation: job title if working (e.g. marketing coordinator), or student / undergrad / grad student if still in school, or the career they want if job hunting. Not the employer name unless that is all they gave.
Their first name or how they asked to be called, if mentioned; otherwise empty string.
Employer or company name if they said it; otherwise empty string.
Annual salary in USD as a whole number if they stated it or a clear range (use midpoint). If not discussed, empty string. Do not fabricate.
Total years in this role or field they mentioned, as digits if possible. If not stated, empty string.
Where they work or live if they said it (e.g. UK, London, Germany, Texas). Empty if unknown. Drives local salary/tuition search.

ISO 4217 code matching how they stated money: GBP if pounds/£/UK salary; EUR for euros; USD for dollars/US. Examples: £60k or 60k in the UK → GBP; $80k US → USD. Default USD only if clearly US or ambiguous dollar.

Total tuition/fees they paid or are paying for the degree (whole number in major units of currency_code). Ask directly if missing. If they only give annual fees, put that number here and set tuition_is_total to false. Empty string if they refuse — backend will still search.
true if tuition_paid is total degree cost; false if it is annual/per-year fees (UK/US scaling is applied server-side). Default true.
